hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a two-storey Commercial building that reflects vernacular interpretations early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s pattern of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a row, the properties have retained their rug-brick cladding, second storey window openings with stone lintels, and brick cornice and parapet. Contextually, each property supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way WB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks & roadway | E-W commercial connector

facade_detail.composition: The street frontage spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys beneath a flat, consistent with its commercial, row typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The visible opening pattern is organized as 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Key storefront and facade features include bay window, decorative brick, original windows.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have retained their rug-brick cladding, second storey window openings with stone lintels, and brick cornice and parapet. Each property has value as a two-storey Commercial building that reflects vernacular interpretations early-twentieth-century architectural styles.

facade_description: Three-storey brick facade with a flat roof and parapet, approximately 5.2 m wide, built flush to the sidewalk. Part of a 1914-1930 commercial row that retains rug-brick cladding, stone lintels at the second storey, and a brick cornice with parapet. A two-storey canted projecting bay window and decorative brickwork enliven the upper facade. The window pattern is 2, 2, 1 bays from ground to third floor, all 1-over-1 double-hung sash. Currently occupied by Jimmy’s Coffee at ground level. No direct photo observation is available; description is based on database and HCD records.
